edges.description: Reduced alpha-galactosidase A activity prevents normal lysosomal clearance of Gb3 and related glycosphingolipids.
Misfolded alpha-galactosidase A variants can trigger ER stress and UPR activation.
edges.target: Endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response

edges.description: Impaired alpha-galactosidase A activity produces accumulation of Gb3 and its deacylated biomarker lyso-Gb3.
The stored substrate burden is reflected clinically by elevated circulating globotriaosylceramide.
The shared lysosomal storage lesion occurs in multiple organ systems and must be interpreted in local tissue contexts.
Gb3 and lyso-Gb3 accumulation promotes inflammatory mediator production.
intermediate_mechanisms: Gb3 and lyso-Gb3 sensing by innate immune cells

edges.description: Kidney and podocyte substrate accumulation defines the renal branch and is the context for renal GL-3 tissue readouts.

edges.description: Cardiac substrate accumulation drives the branch leading to hypertrophic and fibrotic Fabry cardiomyopathy.

edges.description: Ocular substrate deposition defines the corneal and lenticular opacity branch.
Cutaneous vascular substrate accumulation defines the angiokeratoma branch.
Fabry podocyte injury includes lysosomal and autophagy dysfunction.

edges.description: Fabry podocyte models show oxidative-stress and mitochondrial-abnormality pathways downstream of GLA deficiency.

UPR activation causes pro-inflammatory cytokine release and contributes to inflammatory status.

nodes.description: Episodic or chronic abdominal pain resulting from gastrointestinal autonomic dysfunction and ischemia from glycosphingolipid accumulation in gut vasculature and nerves.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002027 | Abdominal pain | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Tingling, burning, or painful paresthesias affecting the extremities, particularly hands and feet, often the earliest clinical manifestation. Pain crises can be disabling. Pathophysiology involves neuronal sensitization from lyso-Gb3 effects and lysosomal dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: The onset of symptoms occurs during childhood, with acroparesthesia, heat intolerance, and gastrointestinal symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and neuropathic pain

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0031006 | Acroparesthesia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Pathogenic GLA variants reduce or abolish α-galactosidase A activity, impairing lysosomal catabolism of neutral glycosphingolipids. This is the upstream enzymatic lesion that explains both the biochemical substrate burden and the genotype-dependent response to chaperone or gene-restorative therapies.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Fabry disease represents an X-linked, multisystem lysosomal storage disorder caused by defective function of the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A (α-Gal A).

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac Involvement in Fabry Disease: JACC Review Topic of the Week.
evidence.snippet: Fabry disease (FD) is a rare X-linked inherited lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficient α-galactosidase A activity

experimental_models.description: The model assays restoration of alpha-galactosidase A activity after GLA modRNA treatment.
experimental_models.name: Fabry patient iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte GLA modRNA rescue model

nodes.description: Small, dark red skin lesions that cluster in the lower trunk and genital region. These are pathognomonic cutaneous manifestations of Fabry disease resulting from vascular dilatation with epidermal hyperkeratosis.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Subsequently, symptoms related to progressive impairment appear, such as angiokeratomas, cornea verticillata, left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, proteinuria, and renal insufficiency

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001014 | Angiokeratoma | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Fabry atrial cardiomyocyte models show altered action-potential behavior, calcium handling, and contraction, and in silico atrial models translate these cellular changes into early P-wave morphology changes and increased atrial fibrillation vulnerability.

computational_models.description: The model translates cellular action-potential and calcium-handling changes into atrial ECG and arrhythmia outputs.
computational_models.name: Bi-atrial in silico Fabry arrhythmia model

evidence.reference_title: Early Atrial Remodeling Drives Arrhythmia in Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Atrial GLA p.N215S iPSC-CMs demonstrated a more positive diastolic membrane potential, faster action potential upstroke velocity, greater incidence of delayed afterdepolarizations, greater contraction force, and alterations in calcium handling compared with wild-type iPSC-CMs.

evidence.reference_title: Early Atrial Remodeling Drives Arrhythmia in Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Simulations with these changes in the in silico models resulted in similar P-wave morphology changes to those seen in early FD cardiomyopathy and increased atrial fibrillation vulnerability.

experimental_models.description: The model assays action-potential, calcium-handling, and contraction changes in Fabry atrial cardiomyocytes.
experimental_models.name: GLA p.N215S iPSC-derived atrial cardiomyocyte arrhythmia model

nodes.description: Lysosomal Gb3 accumulation in Fabry podocytes impairs autophagic flux, leading to accumulation of damaged proteins and organelles. Persistent podocyte injury despite enzyme replacement therapy is mediated in part by α-synuclein accumulation, implicating lysosomal toxicity as a disease driver beyond Gb3 storage.

evidence.reference_title: Accumulation of α-synuclein mediates podocyte injury in Fabry nephropathy.
evidence.snippet: Genetic and pharmacological inhibition of SNCA improved lysosomal structure and function in Fabry podocytes, exceeding the benefits of ERT

nodes.description: Physiological bi-atrial simulation model parameterized with GLA p.N215S iPSC-derived atrial cardiomyocyte changes to evaluate P-wave morphology and atrial fibrillation vulnerability in early Fabry cardiac disease.
nodes.id: Bi-atrial in silico Fabry arrhythmia model

evidence.reference_title: Early Atrial Remodeling Drives Arrhythmia in Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Simulations with these changes in the in silico models resulted in similar P-wave morphology changes to those seen in early FD cardiomyopathy and increased atrial fibrillation vulnerability.

nodes.description: Atrial fibrillation and ventricular arrhythmias result from cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, and inflammatory remodeling that create arrhythmogenic substrate.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac Involvement in Fabry Disease: JACC Review Topic of the Week.
evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular involvement usually manifests as left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, heart failure, and arrhythmias

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0011675 | Arrhythmia | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Gb3 accumulation in cardiac tissue contributes to Fabry cardiomyopathy, with cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, heart failure risk, and arrhythmogenic remodeling. This branch separates cardiac outcome biology from the renal substrate-clearance surrogate.
nodes.id: Cardiac glycosphingolipid storage and myocardial remodeling

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac Involvement in Fabry Disease: JACC Review Topic of the Week.
evidence.snippet: globotriasylceramide (Gb3) in affected tissues, including the heart.

evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular involvement usually manifests as left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, heart failure, and arrhythmias

experimental_models.name: Fabry patient iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte GL-3 clearance model

nodes.description: In cardiac pluripotent-stem-cell models of Fabry disease, GLA deficiency and Gb3 accumulation impair autophagic flux and protein turnover, increase mitochondrial reactive oxygen species, and promote apoptosis/necrosis and hypertrophic cellular features. This node captures a cardiac intracellular mechanism that is not measured by the renal GL-3 surrogate.
nodes.id: Cardiomyocyte autophagy impairment and oxidative stress

evidence.reference_title: Generation of GLA-Knockout Human Embryonic Stem Cell Lines to Model Autophagic Dysfunction and Exosome Secretion in Fabry Disease-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: cardiomyocytes differentiated from these hESCs (GLA-null CMs) were characterized by the accumulation of Gb3 and significant increases of cell surface area, the landmarks of FD-associated cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Generation of GLA-Knockout Human Embryonic Stem Cell Lines to Model Autophagic Dysfunction and Exosome Secretion in Fabry Disease-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: This caused impairment of autophagic flux and protein turnover, resulting in an increase of reactive oxygen species and apoptosis.

experimental_models.description: The model assays GLA-null cardiomyocyte autophagic flux, ROS production, apoptosis, necrosis, and hypertrophic changes.
experimental_models.name: GLA-null hESC-derived cardiomyocyte autophagy and ROS model

nodes.description: Lens opacity/cataract is a frequent ocular manifestation of Fabry disease and is anatomically distinct from corneal opacity.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000518 | Cataract | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: characteristic corneal and lenticular opacities

nodes.description: Progressive renal dysfunction leading to end-stage renal disease. Driven by podocyte loss, glomerulosclerosis, tubulointerstitial fibrosis, and persistent inflammation.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: proteinuria, and renal insufficiency. The latter being the main cause of death in FD.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000083 | Renal insufficiency | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Progressive myocardial storage, hypertrophy, and fibrosis can culminate in congestive heart failure.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001635 | Congestive heart failure | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Dilated conjunctival vessels are part of Fabry ocular vascular involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000524 | Conjunctival telangiectasia | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Whorl-like corneal deposits (cornea verticillata) resulting from glycosphingolipid accumulation in corneal epithelium. Pathognomonic for Fabry disease and detectable on slit-lamp examination.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0500008 | Cornea verticillata | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Corneal opacification from glycosphingolipid deposition in the cornea. Fabry corneal opacities are usually detected on slit-lamp examination and generally do not impair vision.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0007957 | Corneal opacity | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: GL3 accumulation in skin and cutaneous microvasculature contributes to angiokeratomas and other microvascular skin findings, separating the cutaneous branch from renal, cardiac, and neural manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical relevance of globotriaosylceramide accumulation in Fabry disease and the effect of agalsidase beta in affected tissues.
evidence.snippet: Accumulation of GL3 in the kidney, heart, vasculature, neurons, skin, gastrointestinal tract and auditory system correlates to cellular damage and irreversible organ damage

evidence.reference_title: Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: microvascular skin lesions, eg, angiokeratomas

nodes.description: Shortened P-wave duration and PQ interval can appear in Fabry disease before overt cardiomyopathy, indicating early atrial conduction remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: Early Atrial Remodeling Drives Arrhythmia in Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: ECG analysis demonstrated P-wave duration and PQ interval shortening in FD adults before the onset of cardiomyopathy.

nodes.description: Elevated circulating globotriaosylceramide reflects the systemic glycosphingolipid storage burden caused by alpha-galactosidase A deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0033595 | Elevated circulating globotriaosylceramide concentration | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Misfolded α-Gal A variants trigger endoplasmic reticulum stress and activate the unfolded protein response (UPR). Persistent UPR activation causes apoptosis and inflammatory signaling through NF-κB and MAPK pathways, contributing to cell death and tissue injury.
nodes.id: Endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response

evidence.reference_title: The Inflammatory Pathogenetic Pathways of Fabry Nephropathy and Agalopathy: <italic>GLA</italic> Variant Induction of Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress.
evidence.snippet: Missense variants in the coding sequence of the GLA gene would generate the misfolding of the altered protein alpha-galactosidase A. Emergence of misfolded proteins may generate stress of the endoplasmic reticulum, leading to induction of the unfolded protein response (UPR)

nodes.description: Recombinant α-galactosidase A (agalsidase alfa/beta and pegunigalsidase alfa) restores enzyme activity and reduces Gb3 accumulation. Early initiation improves outcomes, but renal injury and cardiac hypertrophy can still progress despite long-term therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Accumulation of α-synuclein mediates podocyte injury in Fabry nephropathy.
evidence.snippet: long-term use of ERT reduced Gb3 accumulation in podocytes but did not reverse podocyte injury

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: development and progression of left ventricular hypertrophy despite long-term enzyme replacement therapy.
evidence.snippet: Over a median follow-up of >10 years under ERT, 36% of the patients still had LVMI cardiac progression, and 32%, predominantly older men, experienced major AFD-related events.

nodes.description: Human Fabry patient iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte model that recapitulates lysosomal GL-3 accumulation in cardiac cells and supports testing tissue-specific substrate-reduction responses outside the renal biopsy compartment.
nodes.id: Fabry patient iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte GL-3 clearance model

evidence.reference_title: Effective clearance of GL-3 in a human iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte model of Fabry disease.
evidence.snippet: This new in vitro model recapitulates essential features of cardiomyocytes from patients with Fabry disease and therefore provides a useful and relevant tool for further investigations of new therapy.

nodes.description: Patient-derived iPSC-cardiomyocyte model used to test lipid-nanoparticle delivery of GLA-encoding modified mRNA, measuring GB3 reduction and lysosomal-protein rescue in cardiac cells.
nodes.id: Fabry patient iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte GLA modRNA rescue model

evidence.reference_title: GLA-modified RNA treatment lowers GB3 levels in iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes from Fabry-affected individuals.
evidence.snippet: Together, our results validate the utility of iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes from affected individuals as a model to study disease processes in Fabry disease and the therapeutic potential of GLA modRNA treatment to reduce GB3 accumulation in the heart.

nodes.description: Female Fabry patient iPSC-cardiomyocyte model exploiting X-chromosome inactivation to produce GLA-deficient disease clones and normal-activity isogenic controls, with automated Gb3 staining for high-content drug screening.
nodes.id: Female Fabry iPSC-cardiomyocyte high-content drug-screening model

evidence.reference_title: Generation of Fabry cardiomyopathy model for drug screening using induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes from a female Fabry patient.
evidence.snippet: We generated a Fabry cardiomyopathy model and a drug screening system by using iPS-CMs from a female Fabry patient.

nodes.description: TGF-β-driven profibrotic signaling leads to extracellular matrix deposition in kidney and heart. This fibrotic process becomes partially independent of Gb3 substrate levels and contributes to progressive organ dysfunction through epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and myofibroblast activation.

biological_processes: transforming growth factor beta signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: Complement activation and cellular inflammation in Fabry disease patients despite enzyme replacement therapy.
evidence.snippet: we found increased IL-6, IL-10 and TGF-ß1 serum levels in FD patients

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac Involvement in Fabry Disease: JACC Review Topic of the Week.
evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular involvement usually manifests as left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, heart failure, and arrhythmias

evidence.reference_title: Genome-wide expression analysis in a Fabry disease human podocyte cell line.
evidence.snippet: Fabry disease (FD) is an X-linked lysosomal disease caused by an enzyme deficiency of alpha-galactosidase A (α-gal A)

evidence.reference_title: Long-read sequencing enables comprehensive molecular genetic diagnosis of Fabry disease.
evidence.snippet: Genetic testing is the gold standard for precise diagnosis of FD, however conventional genetic testing could miss deep intronic variants and large deletions or duplications

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: GLA | galactosidase alpha | hgnc:4296 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: Genome-edited iPSC-derived atrial cardiomyocyte model carrying the GLA p.N215S variant, used to measure Gb3 accumulation, atrial action-potential changes, calcium handling, contraction, and arrhythmia-linked cellular changes.
nodes.id: GLA p.N215S iPSC-derived atrial cardiomyocyte arrhythmia model

evidence.reference_title: Early Atrial Remodeling Drives Arrhythmia in Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Cellular contractile and electrophysiological function were examined in a novel atrial cellular FD model developed and imputed into in silico atrial models to provide insight into mechanisms of arrhythmia.

nodes.description: CRISPR/Cas9 GLA-knockout human embryonic stem cell-derived cardiomyocyte model used to study cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, vesicle trafficking, autophagic flux, mitochondrial ROS production, and cell death.
nodes.id: GLA-null hESC-derived cardiomyocyte autophagy and ROS model

evidence.reference_title: Generation of GLA-Knockout Human Embryonic Stem Cell Lines to Model Autophagic Dysfunction and Exosome Secretion in Fabry Disease-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: To summarize, in the present study we show that CRISPR/Cas9-mediated GLA knockout of hESC-derived CMs can serve as an in vitro FD model for studying hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

nodes.description: AAV and lentiviral-based therapies deliver functional GLA gene for sustained enzyme expression. Shows promise for achieving durable Gb3 clearance and reduced need for repeated infusions.

evidence.reference_title: Progress and Challenges in the Treatment of Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Future therapeutic options may include substrate reduction therapy, gene therapy, messenger RNA therapy, and/or vesicle-packaged enzyme replacement therapy

nodes.description: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss, often high-frequency, resulting from cochlear vascular dysfunction and neuronal damage from glycosphingolipid accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000365 | Hearing impairment | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Intolerance to high ambient temperatures resulting from hypohidrosis and autonomic dysfunction. Frequently triggers or worsens Fabry pain crises.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002046 | Heat intolerance | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Reduced or absent sweating resulting from autonomic nerve fiber dysfunction due to glycosphingolipid accumulation. Often presents in childhood and contributes to heat intolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000966 | Hypohidrosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Gb3/lyso-Gb3 accumulation and unfolded-protein stress activate innate inflammatory programs, including complement activation and monocyte/macrophage/dendritic-cell cytokine release. This node captures the shared inflammatory mediator state before its consequences are interpreted in renal, vascular, and profibrotic tissue contexts.

evidence.reference_title: The Inflammatory Pathogenetic Pathways of Fabry Nephropathy and Agalopathy: <italic>GLA</italic> Variant Induction of Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress.
evidence.snippet: It has been demonstrated that intralysosomal accumulation of Gb3 and LysoGb3 triggers an inflammatory response.

evidence.reference_title: Complement activation and cellular inflammation in Fabry disease patients despite enzyme replacement therapy.
evidence.snippet: we demonstrate strong activation of the complement system in 17 classical male FD patients with either missense or nonsense mutations before and after ERT as evidenced by high C3a and C5a serum levels

evidence.reference_title: Inflammation in Fabry disease: stages, molecular pathways, and therapeutic implications.
evidence.snippet: Inflammation is emerging as an important aspect of the pathogenesis of Fabry disease. This is thought to be predominantly mediated by the innate immune response, with growing evidence pointing towards the potential involvement of adaptive immune mechanisms

nodes.description: Glucosylceramide synthase inhibition is an investigational substrate-reduction strategy for Fabry disease that aims to reduce glycosphingolipid substrate supply. In Fabry iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes, substrate reduction therapy prevented accumulation and cleared lysosomal GL-3, linking the cardiac non-animal model to a candidate therapeutic mechanism.

evidence.reference_title: Effective clearance of GL-3 in a human iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte model of Fabry disease.
evidence.snippet: Using this human in vitro model, we demonstrated that substrate reduction therapy via glucosylceramide synthase inhibition was able to prevent accumulation and to clear lysosomal GL-3 in cardiomyocytes.

nodes.description: Concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle resulting from cardiomyocyte lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, inflammation, and fibrosis. Increases arrhythmia risk and may progress to heart failure.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac Involvement in Fabry Disease: JACC Review Topic of the Week.
evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular involvement usually manifests as left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial fibrosis, heart failure, and arrhythmias, which limit quality of life and represent the most common causes of death

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001712 | Left ventricular hypertrophy | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: development and progression of left ventricular hypertrophy despite long-term enzyme replacement therapy.
evidence.snippet: LVH at treatment initiation was a strong predictor of LVMI progression and adverse events on ERT.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of Anderson-Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Measurement of the biomarkers Gb3 and Lyso Gb3 in biological specimens may facilitate diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: How well does urinary lyso-Gb3 function as a biomarker in Fabry disease?
evidence.snippet: Increased urinary excretion of lyso-Gb(3) of Fabry patients correlated well with a number of indicators of disease severity.

evidence.reference_title: An expert consensus on the recommendations for the use of biomarkers in Fabry disease.
evidence.snippet: Disease-specific biomarkers are useful in the diagnosis of Fabry disease; non-disease-specific biomarkers may be useful to assess organ damage

readouts.interpretation: Increased lyso-Gb3 reflects the upstream sphingolipid substrate accumulation caused by alpha-galactosidase A deficiency.

nodes.description: Deficient α-galactosidase A activity causes progressive lysosomal accumulation of globotriaosylceramide (Gb3/GL-3) and related metabolites including lyso-Gb3. This node captures the shared biochemical storage lesion before it is specialized into organ and cell-type contexts.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the GLA gene lead to the progressive accumulation of globotriaosylceramide (Gb3).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical relevance of globotriaosylceramide accumulation in Fabry disease and the effect of agalsidase beta in affected tissues.
evidence.snippet: Fabry disease (FD) is a rare lysosomal storage disorder, characterized by a reduction in α-galactosidase A enzyme activity and the progressive accumulation of globotriaosylceramide (GL3) and its metabolites in the cells of various organs.

nodes.description: Fabry podocyte models show mitochondrial abnormalities and oxidative-stress pathway activation downstream of GLA deficiency. This captures a renal cellular stress mechanism rather than a whole-disease terminal phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Genome-wide expression analysis in a Fabry disease human podocyte cell line.
evidence.snippet: Functional analysis of differentially expressed genes showed their involvement in various pathways related to oxidative stress

evidence.reference_title: Genome-wide expression analysis in a Fabry disease human podocyte cell line.
evidence.snippet: The complex pathogenesis of FD involves lysosomal dysfunction, altered autophagy, and mitochondrial abnormalities.

nodes.description: Recurrent nausea and vomiting are gastrointestinal manifestations of Fabry disease and accompany the broader autonomic and gastrointestinal symptom burden.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002017 | Nausea and vomiting | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Severe glomerular filtration-barrier injury from Fabry nephropathy can manifest as nephrotic syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000100 | Nephrotic syndrome | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Episodic or chronic neuropathic pain including Fabry crises (burning extremity pain often triggered by fever or exertion) affecting a majority of patients, beginning in childhood or adolescence.

evidence.reference_title: Nervous system manifestations of Fabry disease: data from FOS – the Fabry Outcome Survey.
evidence.snippet: FOS has confirmed a very high frequency of neuropathic pain (76% males, 64% females), beginning in childhood or adolescence.

nodes.description: Glycosphingolipid deposition in ocular tissues produces the ocular branch of Fabry disease, including whorl-like cornea verticillata, corneal opacity, and lenticular opacity/cataract detected on eye examination.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Gb3 accumulates in lysosomes of different types of cells of the heart, kidneys, skin, eyes, central nervous system, and gastrointestinal system

evidence.reference_title: Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: Ocular involvement includes corneal and lenticular abnormalities

nodes.description: Glycosphingolipid accumulation in peripheral sensory and autonomic pathways contributes to small-fiber dysfunction, neuropathic pain, impaired sweating, heat intolerance, and gastrointestinal symptoms.
nodes.id: Peripheral small-fiber and autonomic glycosphingolipid storage

evidence.reference_title: Clinical relevance of globotriaosylceramide accumulation in Fabry disease and the effect of agalsidase beta in affected tissues.
evidence.snippet: Accumulation of GL3 in the kidney, heart, vasculature, neurons, skin, gastrointestinal tract and auditory system correlates to cellular damage and irreversible organ damage

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease: genetics, pathology, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: The onset of symptoms occurs during childhood, with acroparesthesia, heat intolerance, and gastrointestinal symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and neuropathic pain

nodes.description: Migalastat stabilizes GLA mutant variants and improves lysosomal trafficking. Useful for approximately 35-50% of patients with amenable mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Progress and Challenges in the Treatment of Fabry Disease.
evidence.snippet: if a responding ('amenable') α-galactosidase A mutation is present, oral pharmacological chaperone therapy (migalastat 123 mg, every other day)

nodes.description: Urinary protein excretion, often the earliest sign of Fabry nephropathy. Progresses from microalbuminuria to overt proteinuria. Results from podocyte injury due to Gb3/lyso-Gb3 accumulation, autophagy impairment, and inflammatory signaling.

evidence.reference_title: Genome-wide expression analysis in a Fabry disease human podocyte cell line.
evidence.snippet: This study focuses on genome-wide expression analysis in an FD human podocyte model to gain insights into the underlying mechanisms of podocyte dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000093 | Proteinuria | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Significant improvement in Fabry disease podocytopathy after 3 years of treatment with agalsidase beta.
evidence.snippet: Despite normal renal function and urinalysis, renal biopsy showed abnormal structure, with marked accumulation of GL-3 in podocytes, partial effacement of foot processes and irregularly reduced expression of nephrin in the slit diaphragm.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical relevance of globotriaosylceramide accumulation in Fabry disease and the effect of agalsidase beta in affected tissues.
evidence.snippet: Treatment with agalsidase beta is effective in substantially clearing GL3 in a range of cells from the tissues affected by FD.

readouts.interpretation: Renal GL-3 inclusions are a tissue-level readout of the kidney/podocyte storage branch and can decrease when enzyme replacement clears GL-3.
readouts.target: Renal glycosphingolipid storage and podocyte injury

nodes.description: GL-3 accumulation in kidney cells, especially podocytes, injures the glomerular filtration barrier. Podocyte storage connects the shared Fabry substrate burden to renal GL-3 biopsy readouts, proteinuria, and progressive chronic kidney disease.
nodes.id: Renal glycosphingolipid storage and podocyte injury

evidence.reference_title: Significant improvement in Fabry disease podocytopathy after 3 years of treatment with agalsidase beta.
evidence.snippet: renal biopsy showed abnormal structure, with marked accumulation of GL-3 in podocytes, partial effacement of foot processes and irregularly reduced expression of nephrin in the slit diaphragm.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical relevance of globotriaosylceramide accumulation in Fabry disease and the effect of agalsidase beta in affected tissues.
evidence.snippet: Damage leads to renal dysfunction and end-stage renal disease

nodes.description: Increased risk of ischemic stroke and transient ischemic attacks due to cerebrovascular dysfunction from endothelial dysfunction, microvascular remodeling, and pro-thrombotic state.

evidence.reference_title: When and How to Diagnose Fabry Disease in Clinical Practice.
evidence.snippet: Symptoms and organ involvements of classical Fabry disease are acral pain crisis, cornea verticillata, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, stroke and chronic kidney disease with proteinuria.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001297 | Stroke | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Management of complications including pain control, renal protection with ACE inhibitors, cardiac monitoring, and stroke prevention. Pain management and cardiovascular monitoring are essential for quality of life.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry Disease: Current and Novel Therapeutic Strategies. A Narrative Review.
evidence.snippet: A multidisciplinary symptomatic treatment is recommended for FD patients, personalized according to disease manifestations and their severity.

nodes.description: Ringing or buzzing perception in the ears that accompanies Fabry cochleo-vestibular involvement and can occur with hearing impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Fabry disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000360 | Tinnitus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: The same lysosomal substrate burden occurs across kidney, heart, blood vessels, neurons, skin, ocular tissues, and other affected compartments. This branch point represents the generic multisystem storage state before downstream damage is specialized by organ location, cell type, and local stress-response biology.

nodes.description: Transient episodes of focal neurological deficit due to cerebrovascular dysfunction. Often precedes completed stroke and reflects the underlying vasculopathy of Fabry disease.

evidence.snippet: Thus, 13.2% (15.1% males, 11.5% females) have suffered an ischaemic stroke or transient ischaemic attack, usually at an early age.

nodes.description: Gb3/lyso-Gb3 accumulation in endothelial and vascular cells leads to adhesion-molecule upregulation, reduced nitric oxide availability, and vascular remodeling. This branch links multisystem storage to cerebrovascular events and cochleo-vestibular manifestations.
nodes.id: Vascular endothelial glycosphingolipid storage and dysfunction
